title: A converse theorem of Gross-Zagier and Kolyvagin: CM case
speaker: Ye Tian
abstract: Let $E$ be a CM elliptic curves over rationals and $p$ an odd prime ordinary for $E$. If the $\mathbb Z_p$ corank of $p^\infty$ Selmer group for $E$ equals one, then we show that the analytic rank of $E$ also equals one. This is joint work with Ashay Burungale.

proof_stages.label: BSD rank-one package and the missing converse
proof_stages.summary: The talk formulates the rank-one BSD equivalence among analytic rank one, Mordell-Weil rank one with finite Sha, and p-primary Selmer corank one, recalls Gross-Zagier--Kolyvagin for the forward direction and the exact-sequence implication to Selmer rank, and identifies the converse Selmer-rank-to-analytic-rank implication as the target, especially because existing non-CM methods require big image hypotheses excluding CM curves.

proof_stages.label: CM converse theorem and BSD consequences
proof_stages.summary: For a CM elliptic curve over Q and an ordinary prime p≠2, the main theorem states that p-primary Selmer corank one implies analytic rank one, and when combined with known CM BSD formula results it gives the p-part BSD formula; the speaker also records variants for CM modular forms and ongoing extensions to p=2 and supersingular cases.

proof_stages.label: Choice of relevant Heegner data in the CM field
proof_stages.summary: To make Heegner points usable in the CM setting, the speaker takes the Heegner quadratic field to be the CM field K itself, compensates for the sign by a quadratic twist with nonzero central value, rewrites the data as a self-dual Rankin-Selberg pair (g,χ), and constructs the relevant Heegner point p0 on an auxiliary abelian variety A via Shimura-curve parametrizations.

proof_stages.label: Reduction to non-torsion of the constructed Heegner point
proof_stages.summary: The desired analytic rank-one conclusion for L(E,s) is reduced, using the factorization of L(g,χ) and the Gross-Zagier formula, to proving that the constructed point p0 in A(K) is nonzero after tensoring with Q, while the original Selmer-corank-one hypothesis is transferred to the corresponding Selmer group of A over K.

proof_stages.label: Anticyclotomic Iwasawa family of Heegner points
proof_stages.summary: The proof passes to the anticyclotomic Zp-extension K∞/K, forms the compact Selmer module S over the Iwasawa algebra, uses nonvanishing of almost all twisted L-values together with Gross-Zagier and Kolyvagin arguments to show S has rank one, and packages higher-conductor Heegner points into a compatible big Heegner class κ∈S.

proof_stages.label: Heegner-point main conjecture and descent
proof_stages.summary: The crucial Iwasawa-theoretic input is a CM Heegner-point main conjecture equating characteristic ideals involving S/κ and the torsion part of the dual Selmer module X; once this is known, a descent/control argument from the corank-one hypothesis forces the specialization κ0, hence p0, to be non-torsion.

proof_stages.label: Proof of the CM Heegner-point main conjecture
proof_stages.summary: The main conjecture is proved by factoring the two-variable Rankin-Selberg p-adic L-function into Katz p-adic L-functions in the CM case, differentiating along the cyclotomic direction, using p-adic Gross-Zagier to identify the Rankin derivative with the Heegner regulator, using elliptic-unit/Rubin main-conjecture results to identify the Katz factor with the relevant Selmer characteristic ideal, and invoking nonvanishing of the regulator to conclude the desired characteristic-ideal identity.

examples.name: Ordinary prime p=3 for a CM elliptic curve
examples.note: The speaker uses the curve y^2=x^3+4x^2+2x, with CM by a quadratic imaginary field in which 3 splits, to illustrate how the CM converse theorem can turn information about small Selmer groups in a quadratic-twist family into analytic-rank information.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker applies the ordinary-prime criterion to the concrete CM curve, noting that p=3 splits in the CM field and hence is ordinary for that curve

examples.name: Twists p y^2=x^3-x for primes p congruent to 5 mod 8
examples.note: This family is presented as a concrete corollary/application in which the three rank-one conditions and the full BSD formula hold, and it is later reused to clarify the quadratic-twist notation in the Heegner-point construction.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker reasons through the twist family p y^2=x^3-x for p≡5 mod 8, explaining how Heegner-point non-torsion, Gross–Zagier/Kolyvagin, and local BSD arguments give rank one and the BSD formula
